narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 52% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your profile, including your Year 1 school decile rank of 4 (below the typical student's 5.71), your Year 3 decile of 3 (lower than the average 5.53), an LSAT score of 28 (well below the dataset average of 36.7), a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.60 (above the average 3.21), upper-middle family income, being female, Hispanic background, and planning part-time work. Compared to typical students in our data—who often have higher school decile ranks, much stronger LSAT scores, are mostly white and full-time—your profile showed some standout strengths but more risks in key areas. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile rank, also pushing in the same direction. Your LSAT score was the third biggest influence, again contributing toward the failure prediction due to how low it is relative to peers. Family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, study workload, and gender followed in that order of influence, each slightly tilting the odds toward failure as well, even your above-average GPA. No single factor was overwhelmingly decisive, but together these created a close call at 52% risk. It's empathetic to note that minorities like Hispanic students and part-time planners are less common in the data, which may amplify perceived risks unfairly. To boost your future bar passing chances, focus on retaking the LSAT for a higher score, as that's a key predictor you can directly improve with preparation. Also, prioritize full-time study if possible to match the typical successful profile, and excel in law school coursework early to lift your decile ranks. With targeted effort on these changeable areas, you could shift your predicted odds significantly in a future application. Keep pushing—you have a solid GPA foundation to build on.
